statement: If V3 analysis is organized around matched sample-level deltas within each family (plus ranked instability/sensitivity tables), then a small set of robust, reportable findings will emerge quickly and will be more stable than findings obtained by browsing aggregate charts. Distance-map visualizations (clustered heatmaps, MDS) will be useful for discovery but will require validation via matched deltas and driver decomposition.
rationale: The V3 matrix has shared sample ordinals across experiments and strong family structure, which enables paired comparisons. Prior pilots emphasize geometry shifts and confounds; ranked instability and matched deltas are designed to surface these shifts systematically while mitigating forking-path bias. Clustering/MDS are standard exploratory tools for distance matrices but are sensitive to metric scaling and thus need explicit validation.
keyAssumptions: Sample ordinals are truly comparable across experiments (same underlying evidence sets / bundle construction per sample).
Derived per-sample metrics (abstain rate, singleton rate, expected stage, subset size, conflict) capture the main behavioral changes of interest.
Within-family contrasts are defined cleanly enough to support paired deltas (baseline and variant are comparable aside from the intended intervention).
Distance metrics (e.g., JS divergence on per-sample expected-stage distributions) are not dominated by a single metric artifact.
validationCriteria: A mining script can rank the top 5-10 family effects by paired effect size with bootstrap/permutation uncertainty, and these effects remain qualitatively similar under reasonable alternative aggregations (median vs mean, trimming, excluding top-driver evidence).
Sample-instability rankings are reproducible across different metric choices (e.g., expected-stage variance vs abstain variance) and lead to consistent case-study candidates.

Experiment distance clustering/MDS yields stable coarse groupings under small perturbations (metric standardization choices, alternative distance definitions), or else is explicitly flagged as unstable and not used for claims.
critiquePoints: If bundle-size families change the effective unit (120 vs 600 responses), naive deltas could be incomparable without per-sample normalization.
High TBM/closed-world conflict values may saturate and reduce interpretability; mining should not over-index on conflict alone.
Matched deltas can still be misleading if the intervention changes sample composition (e.g., different bundling), so driver decomposition is needed.
questions: What is the canonical baseline per family (e.g., d1 control, a1 abstain_false, a2 l2 vs l3)?
Should the primary severity metric be expected stage, modal stage, or a calibrated continuous score derived from BetP?
How should abstentions be treated in severity comparisons: excluded, mapped to contradiction, or modeled separately?
